claim: readJsonFile silently returns {} on JSON parse errors, causing saveSettings to overwrite the entire settings file with only RTK data, destroying all other extension settings
evidence: function readJsonFile(filePath: string): Record<string, unknown> { try { if (!fs.existsSync(filePath)) return {}; return JSON.parse(fs.readFileSync(filePath, 'utf8')); } catch { return {}; } }
runtimeTrigger: If settings.json is temporarily corrupted (e.g., partial write by another process, disk full during write, or manual edit error), readJsonFile returns {}. Then saveSettings calls readJsonFile, gets {}, sets settings['rtk'] = config, and writes back {"rtk": ...}, permanently destroying all other extensions' settings that were in the file.

claim: commandMap grows unbounded when tool_call events have no matching tool_result (cancelled/aborted tool calls), causing memory leak within long sessions
evidence: state.commandMap.set(event.toolCallId, { command, toolName: 'bash' });  // In createToolCallHandler — entries are only deleted in tool_result handler via state.commandMap.delete(event.toolCallId)
runtimeTrigger: When a tool call is aborted/cancelled by the user or the agent before a tool_result is emitted, the commandMap entry is never cleaned up. Over a long coding session with many cancelled operations, this leaks memory. The map is only cleared on session_start/switch/fork/shutdown, not on individual tool call cancellations.

claim: generateFilename uses seconds-precision local time, causing file overwrite when the same command produces tee output twice within the same second
evidence: function generateFilename(command: string): string { const now = new Date(); ... const slug = slugify(command); return `${date}_${time}_${slug}.txt`; }
runtimeTrigger: If a filter matches an errored command and tee is enabled, and the same command (e.g., 'npm test') errors twice within the same wall-clock second (possible with fast test runs or rapid retries), the second saveTee call generates an identical filename and writeFileSync silently overwrites the first file, losing its content.

claim: Singleton openDb caches the first connection and silently returns it for all subsequent calls regardless of the dbPath argument, returning the wrong database
evidence: export function openDb(dbPath?: string): Database.Database { if (db) return db; ... db = new Database(resolvedPath); ... return db; }
runtimeTrigger: If any code path calls openDb(':memory:') before the main session initialization calls openDb() (default path), all subsequent openDb() calls return the in-memory database instead of the persistent on-disk database. This causes all tracking data to be silently lost. While the production flow calls closeDb() between sessions, any plugin or test that opens a connection first corrupts the singleton for the process lifetime until closeDb() is called.
